Hi everyone, I've got the ready to send pull request which includes Python conceptual blending algorithm. I have some questions about unit test overhead, co-authorship with Keyvan, and scheme module loading.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+some thoughts on tensorflow and opencog (150f43b1f88cd078)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: some thoughts on tensorflow and opencog
Messages: 4
Date range: 2015-11-11 to 2015-11-12
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150f43b1f88cd078

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-11

Some quasi-random thoughts on tensorflow. Regarding the relationship btw tensorflow and OpenCog -- most simply, an OpenCog Atom is a persistent store of information, whereas a TensorFlow graph is a collection of Operations.

In terms of OpenCog integration, the most straightforward thing would be to implement TensorNode and GroundedSchemaNodes that wrap up TensorFlow Operations. This would allow us to basically embed TensorFlow graphs inside the Atomspace.

Now if one considered tensorflow as a general dataflow approach rather than just a deep learning architecture, one could potentially use it for more than just perception processing.

-- ben

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-11

Not having read the paper nor studied the API... but you use the words 'tensorflow graph' and the atomspace is a graph database, so the declarative knowledge is the shape of the tensorflow graph. The tensors themselves sound like they are ephemeral, and would NOT be something we'd ever store in the atomspace.

What am I missing?

--linas

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-11

BTW vectors and tensors are special cases of a more general non-symmetric category. The more general case is what we use for link-grammar. The interesting theoretical advance would be to make tensorflow work for the non-symmetric generalization (i.e for LG disjuncts). See pregroup grammar.

--linas

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-12

In the case of a standard deep learning network, there could be one tensorflow node (Operation) for each of the 8 or 10 layers. In typical uses of tensorflow, the actual patterns in images are in the tensors, and the nodes just provide high-level organization of tensor operations.

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Student who has interest in GSoC 2015 (14be8c49d81970b2)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Student who has interest in GSoC 2015
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-03-02 to 2015-03-07
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14be8c49d81970b2

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: DongMin Kim
Date: 2015-03-02

Im CS student interested in GSoC 2015 from south korea. I found OpenCog Framework and studied it. I have questions about GSoC projects: Cognitive API, Attention Allocation, Blending, and Minecraft interface.

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-05

DongMin, I get the feeling that Blending might be a good project for you. It involves implementing a MindAgent that creates concept blends combining different concepts in the AtomSpace.

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-03-05

If youre interested in the Minecraft integration idea, a good place to start would be to read the thesis paper written by Jonas Kemper.

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: DongMin Kim
Date: 2015-03-05

Thanks for the advice. I think I probably understood the Blending project. Ill read the papers.

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: DongMin Kim
Date: 2015-03-06

I read the paper on Conceptual Blending. I should implement the MindAgent which performs blending in AtomSpace. May I implement in C++? Can I use Interaction Information or Surprisingness?

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-06

Sounds good! Using C++ is the easiest way. Yes, Interaction Information or other information-theoretic methods work. The same basic mathematics Shujing used should be applicable to blending.

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: DongMin Kim
Date: 2015-03-06

I made a simple plan: 1. Select ConceptNodes 2. Prepare 3. Check conflict 4. Merge links 5. Process remaining conflicts 6. Put new ConceptNode in AtomSpace.

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-07

I think your overall work plan makes sense. Of course there are lots of gaps, but they can be filled in once the basic software structure for the blending agent is there.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Surprisingness_II evaluation in Pattern Miner in our AtomSpace (14b13679dc0863b3)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Surprisingness_II evaluation in Pattern Miner in our AtomSpace
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-01-22 to 2015-01-23
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b13679dc0863b3

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Shujing Ke
Date: 2015-01-22

Hi, Ben & anyone who has interest, I finished the Surpringness_II evaluation in Pattern Miner. The results on the small test corpus are as good as expected.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-23

Thanks Shujing -- This is very interesting... I think your broad concept of a superpattern is OK. It would be good to have these docs on the wiki site. So it now seems that scalability is perhaps the largest concern now, right? The bio databases involve fairly large Atomspaces.

ben

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-23

Eddie/Mike -- could you give Shujing a decent estimate of the size of an Atomspace containing the top 100 MOSES models from 10 MOSES runs plus the GO and MSigB databases? We are trying to get a sense for how big a pattern mining problem this is going to be.

I would say it probably makes sense to prioritize making it sort of distributed.

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Testing ECAN (14b555c5493abbf2)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Testing ECAN
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-02-04 to 2015-02-06
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b555c5493abbf2

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-02-04

Misgana, Matt, etc.,

Here is a relatively simple example for testing/tuning attention allocation. The idea is to get ECAN to emulate a word-co-occurrence based 'word priming effect.'

What we want is:
1) Words in current sentence get high STI (in Attentional Focus)
2) When a new sentence comes in, prior sentences fade from the AF
3) Two words that occurred in AF together get a HebbianLink
4) Importance spreading causes priming: if W and X frequently co-occurred, when W enters AF, X gets importance spread to it

-- Ben

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Misgana Bayetta
Date: 2015-02-06

Hi Ben, What kind of sentence database are we looking for?

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-02-06

Just using a single pages from Simple English Wikipedia should be fine for this purpose...

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Text Understanding from Scratch (14b6245e40bc4e77)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Text Understanding from Scratch
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-02-06 to 2015-03-05
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b6245e40bc4e77

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-02-06

Hi Linas,

You may find this new paper from LeCun and Zhang interesting in relation to the ongoing language learning project:
http://arxiv.org/abs/1502.01710

Thanks,
Cosmo

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-05

Hi Cosmo,

So I skimmed it just now. Its interesting. The telling results are tables 4, 6, 7, 9, 11, 13 where they compare to bag-of-words results. The good news is that they're always better than bag-of-words, which sets a baseline for minimum acceptable performance.

I spent 2013 tweaking moses and beating bag-of-words. The primary trick that I stumbled over is that, by also using very carefully selected word-pairs, one could beat bag-of-word results by a solid, strong margin. My selection criteria was to use high-MI word pairs.

This result made a light-bulb go off in my head. If one can beat bag-of-words by including basic linguistic structure (i.e. [...]

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+To contribute in OpenCog (14f8a5060e96c89b)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: To contribute in OpenCog
Messages: 3
Date range: 1 Sep 2015 to 2015-09-01
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f8a5060e96c89b

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: rishi shah
Date: 1 Sep 2015

Hi folks, I am a student of computer science interested in AI. I wanted to contribute in OpenCog. Can someone tell me how?

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-01

The wiki ideas page is still valid. There are also lots of bugs that need fixing. Current efforts focus on: reasoning using PLN with the forward/backward chainers, getting a working chatbot, continuing integration with ROS for robots and minecraft.

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-01

Three excellent self-contained projects: implement cog-delete in the SQL database (https://github.com/opencog/atomspace/issues/255), fix the handle/atoms confusion in python (issues/201), add generic truth value support to SQL (issues/195).

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Truthvalues are set to (-nan 0) after parsing (1506e6c9a54eba92)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Truthvalues are set to (-nan 0) after parsing
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-10-15 to 2015-10-22
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1506e6c9a54eba92

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: Tareq Alkhaldi
Date: 2015-10-15

So running a recent copy of opencog, I connect to the server, I open scm, I load deduction.scm, do (process_query "Tareq" "Raj is a man."), then run (cog-bind pln-rule-deduction) and the result shows InheritanceLinks with (stv -nan 0). Does anyone know why the truthvalues are being set to -nan 0?

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-15

Dont know; one of the PLN formulas must be dividing by zero. The output format is incorrect, too, it should be using DefinedLinguisticConceptNode not ConceptNode. See http://wiki.opencog.org/w/RelEx_OpenCog_format for the correct format.

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: William Ma
Date: 2015-10-15

From your result it looks like for some reason it is using some old code in opencog/nlp/scm/type-definitions.scm which is where those weird (ConceptNode "DefinedLinguisticConcept-NounNumber") come from. [...]

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-15

The point of having type-definitions.scm is to be able to say that a (DefinedLinguisticConceptNode "verb") is a kind of (ConceptNode "DefinedLinguisticConcept-PartOfSpeech"). But yes, running PLN on it does not make sense. How can we store information that "a verb is a part of speech" yet prevent pln from deducing that "run" is a "part of speech"? A is a B and B is a C does not always mean A is a C... "100 pence is a pound" and "a pound is 454 grams" does not mean "100 pence is 454 grams".

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: Tareq Alkhaldi
Date: 2015-10-20

I removed type-definitions.scm from the list in .conf and it no longer connects the weird stuff, but the TVs are still strange. When are the TVs set when I enter a sentence like "Raj is a man" and it goes through r2l? It doesnt have a truth value until I run pln or set TVs manually? Also, to use the [...]

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-21

The truth value is there; it is set to the default; the printing rule is that the default TV is never printed (to avoid clutter).

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: Tareq Alkhaldi
Date: 2015-10-22

Oh.. what is the default value of a truth value? is it (0.98888 0.98888)? And how can I forcefully print the truth value of an InheritanceLink?

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: Tareq Alkhaldi
Date: 2015-10-22

Okay, I think I found out how to print the tv of a link. Using relex-parse "Socrates is a man", then (cog-tv (cog-link InheritanceLink x y)) gives (stv 1 0). So, is (1 0) the default value for a truth value?

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Trying to use ExampleAgent (14e47bab86eb00fc)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Trying to use ExampleAgent

cheers:-)

--- Message 3/16 ---
From: Gavriel Loria
Date: 1 Jul 2015

Okay, well that seemed to work.

Now, how do I get an example agent working? There are some pre-loaded 
agents in the OpenCog server... Suppose I start one. Then what happens? (I 
went through the Qt creation tutorial, and had a non-crashing build, but I 
had trouble finding the .so file to load in. [...]

--- Message 4/16 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 2 Jul 2015

https://github.com/opencog/opencog/blob/master/examples/agents/README

--- Message 5/16 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-03

Why are you building mind-agents? What does this have to do with your
work?  I was assuming that you would be coding in python or scheme, and
building parser-type things ... which has nothing to do with mind-agents...

--linas

--- Message 6/16 ---
From: Gavriel Loria
Date: 3 Jul 2015

I think I misunderstood the scope of the project. I mistakenly interpreted 
'Implement Simplified Word Grammar Parser' as 'Implement Simplified Word 
Grammar'. So I concluded on my own that it would be necessary to understand 
Attention to identify which parts of the AtomSpace network would be 
 [.. [...]

--- Message 7/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-04

Hi Gavriel,

I see, you were thinking to implement a simplified WG lexicon.  That
would also be very interesting, yet is not the project I was thinking
of...

Understanding attention is relevant to the "simplified WG-style parser" project

However, I suppose implementation-wise, using attention-spreading is
not necessarily the best place to start.   Rather, it would be an
efficiency improvement (a big and perhaps necessary one).   First I
think you could implement a simplified WG-style parser, in the
Atomspace, which is inefficient and doesn't use attention values for
pruning -- but does the job according to the right dynamics, albeit
very slowly...

No worries, understanding WG is definitely not a waste of your time !!

Well it may scare you more than enlighten you, but...

 [...]

--- Message 8/16 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-05

Hi Gavriel,

To simplify your getting started, there are several places where you can
find useful parts that you can steal and use:

https://github.com/opencog/opencog/tree/master/opencog/nlp/lg-dict  has
code to open and read the LG dictionaries and import (parts of) them as
atoms in the atomspace.   William Ma knows this code well and can help. It
should "just plain work".

I've tried twice to do what Ben describes (Its doable, but I ran out of
time). The first attempt is here:
https://github.com/opencog/link-grammar/tree/master/viterbi

and the second here:
https://github.com/opencog/opencog/tree/master/opencog/nlp/viterbi

The first attempt is far more complete; it could actually parse very simple
sentences. It did not use the atomspace, but something that was similar to
 [...]

--- Message 9/16 ---
From: Gavriel Loria
Date: 6 Jul 2015

Hi,

a. How do I execute the first link?
b. Is there relevance to looking at the last link (the second attempt)?

Thanks.

--- Message 10/16 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-07

Its part of opencog. you'd have to build opencog using the usual steps for
that.   Anyway, it is not an executable, it is a library, its a set of
functions for loading parts of the LG dictionary into the atomspace. I
assume that there are examples somewhere of how to do this.  William Ma
knows more.

Perhaps there is something inspirational in there, perhaps not.

--linas

--- Message 11/16 ---
From: Gavriel Loria
Date: 7 Jul 2015

Hi,

I tried using ./configure --enable-viterbi in relex/link-grammar-5.2.5. It 
worked, but I received an error message when making:

error: #error This file requires compiler and library support for the ISO 
C++ 2011 standard. This support is currently experimental, and must be 
 [...]

--- Message 12/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-08

Hi Gavriel,

About RelEx -- that's a good question, and a tough one...

Right now the link parser and relex are run outside OpenCog, and then
RelEx exports data into OpenCog which is transformed by Relex2Logic
into more abstracted semantic links and nodes...

If your GSoC project succeeds, then we'll have a link-parser
alternative running inside OpenCog (i.e. using the Atomspace as a data
structure) ... which will leave relex in an awkward position (as we'll
have link-parsing and R2L inside OpenCog but not relex!)

The natural thing to do then would be to port relex into the
Atomspace, which in principle is much more straightforward than
porting the link parser into the Atomspace (because relex is
algorithmically simple, there's no constraint-satisfaction involved
 [...]

--- Message 13/16 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-08

Don't use the --enable-viterbi flag.  You don't need it -- It doesn't do
anything interesting ... I mostly was just trying to point you at the
viterbi code because you could steal ideas and source code from there.

If you don't use --enable-viterbi, these error messages will go way.  You
will need to say "make clean" first, the configure gain, then make.

In the main relex directory, there is a sell scripte called
"opencog-srever.sh"  It starts up both link grammar and relex, waits for
sentences to be sent to port number 4444, which are then parsed, and
automatically sent to the opencog server, where they "magically" show up in
the atomspace.  They are sent as "atomese" to port 17001, which is where
the cogserver is normally listening.  Ben and others sometimes use the word
 [...]

--- Message 14/16 ---
From: Gavriel Loria
Date: 8 Jul 2015

I suppose my next question then is what is the difference between the 
intent attempts at a viterbi parser you have made and the intent of the 
simplified WG-like parser?

Thanks,
Gavriel

--- Message 15/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-09

At the high level I think the intent is quite similar ---- to make a
more cognitively realistic , AGI-friendly parser using the LG
dictionary and operating within the Atomspace

Compared to Linas, I have studied WG a bit more and have a more
definite interest in exploring WG-style parsing (such as default
inference based on landmark transitivity) in the context of a
"cognitively realistic" parser...

-- ben

--- Message 16/16 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-08

Hi Gavriel,

I'll let Ben answer that, but I can tell you what I learned along the way.

I got interested in a Viterbi-like algo for multiple reasons, some of
which apply to you.

-- I wanted to parse a word-at-a-time, so that I would have a set of
candidate parses handy after each word or sound was heard.

-- I wanted to have a (probabilistic) ranking of the parses from most
to least likely.

-- I wanted to do it in the atomspace, so that other algorithms could
participate in the parsing and the ranking ... for example, reasoning,
reference resolution, pronoun resolution could raise or lower the
ranking of the different parses. Or, in your case, one could use WG
principles to do ... whatever.  Provide guidance and steering to the
main "dumb" parser. [...]

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Turning PLN logical rules into facts (151006bac32477da)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Turning PLN logical rules into facts
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-11-13 to 2015-11-13
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151006bac32477da

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-11-13

Hi, I've just realized that it's a lot easier to represent logical/crisp rules as higher order facts, and use more basic PLN rules like instantiation and deduction to manipulate those facts.

Describing the rules as higher order facts makes them so much more compact. This may narrow down the PLN rule set to the essential.

Nil

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-11-13

Strictly speaking I should speak of an ExtensionalEquivalence. But I don't think it hurts much to use a mixed equivalence, since the TV is <1, 1>.

Nil

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Two small sized projects, doable in 10 days (14b4534d78f65e12)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Two small sized projects, doable in 10 days
Messages: 7
Date range: 2015-02-01 to 2015-02-03
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b4534d78f65e12

============================================================

--- Message 1/7 ---
From: Keyvan Mir Mohammad Sadeghi
Date: 2015-02-01

I need two very-small-sized tasks to test a couple of hires with. Do we have such a thing?

--- Message 2/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-02-02

How small are you thinking?

--- Message 3/7 ---
From: Keyvan Mir Mohammad Sadeghi
Date: 2015-02-02

On the scale of being doable in 10 days for a noob. Python bugs maybe? Would be nice if we could have a list of 4 or 5 issues to choose from.

--- Message 4/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-02-02

If the goal is to get them up to speed on OpenCog, it may be better for them to spend a couple weeks just reading about the system and going through online tutorials... and then do some simple tasks after that...

--- Message 5/7 ---
From: Keyvan Mir Mohammad Sadeghi
Date: 2015-02-02

The initial goal is to test their competency, they will secure a contract if they prove to be autonomous.

--- Message 6/7 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-02-03

I might have a suggestion: Generating scheme-like atom constructors for C++, based on atom_types.script. The goal is to get for C++ the kind of elegant way to create/recall a hypergraph similar to scheme. It would be nice if some constructor could automatically be added to the atomspace API. Once the method is figured out, I think it's doable in 10 days by a noob.

--- Message 7/7 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-03

Nil, macro processing languages. You don't need m4 or hygienic macros, you just need cpp. See the BooleanUTest which uses #define an as->addNode and #define al as->addLink. Keyvan: try going through the list of open issues in opencog. Fixing existing bugs is a good way to learn the internals.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Unified Rules Engine (14fcf242f0030fc0)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Unified Rules Engine
Messages: 3
Date range: 14 Sep 2015 to 16 Sep 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14fcf242f0030fc0

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Fredy
Date: 14 Sep 2015

Can someone point me to source files implementing URE? Are there any sample implementations to use as a reference? I'm designing a new Pathfinder based on OCPlanner.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Misgana Bayetta
Date: 2015-09-15

Hi Fredy, The URE code is located at https://github.com/opencog/atomspace/tree/master/opencog/rule-engine and the specification document is found here http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/URE_Chainer_Design

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Jan Matusiewicz
Date: 16 Sep 2015

There seems to be a small mistake in the document. At the beginning of Rule Selection paragraph: 'How to choose a rule capable of yielding T as input?' But perhaps you meant 'as output?' Because it is a backward-chainer.
